message:
fix(fcp-streaming): reconnect floor + tighter ceiling + ±20% jitter (br-y54mi)

Close the reconnect-storm hole in fcp-streaming's backoff machinery.
Three normative tightenings land together because they're all part
of the same y54mi "don't let reconnect loops turn into thundering
herds OR hot retry storms" rollout.

## 1. New MIN_RECONNECT_DELAY floor (100ms)

A caller that constructed a WsConfig with initial_delay = Duration::
ZERO (or any sub-millisecond value, e.g. propagated from an operator
typo or a downstream default that got set too aggressively) would
previously see delay_for_attempt return Duration::ZERO for attempt 0,
which collapsed the reconnect loop into a hot CPU-spinning busy
retry against a downed upstream. The upstream never saw
load-shedding relief; the local process never saw a reasonable
failure cadence.

New pub const MIN_RECONNECT_DELAY = Duration::from_millis(100) in
src/lib.rs. Floor is applied inside delay_for_attempt():

    let floor = MIN_RECONNECT_DELAY.min(self.max_delay);
    Duration::from_secs_f64(clamped)
        .max(floor)
        .min(self.max_delay)

Floor-then-ceiling ordering matters: if a caller explicitly pinned
max_delay BELOW MIN_RECONNECT_DELAY, the ceiling wins (we respect
explicit caps rather than violating them to satisfy a defensive
floor). Otherwise every wait is at least 100ms, which is short
enough to feel responsive but long enough to avoid starving the
scheduler or DDoSing a recovering upstream.

## 2. MAX_RECONNECT_DELAY tightened 60s → 30s

The previous 60s ceiling let a repeatedly-failing upstream park a
reconnecting client in a one-attempt-per-minute pattern that masked
outage observability — operators looking at dashboards couldn't tell
a healthy-but-slow reconnect from a stuck connector. 30s is still
generous enough to back off meaningfully from an overloaded upstream
but tight enough that the operator-facing "is this connector
working?" signal remains legible.

## 3. Jitter tightened ±50% → ±20%

The old jitter path was `random_float().mul_add(1.0, 0.5)`, which
maps U[0, 1] to U[0.5, 1.5] — a ±50% band. That's wider than
necessary for thundering-herd defense: two clients reconnecting to
the same upstream need *some* phase drift to avoid locking to the
same retry tick, but they don't need the fast drift-apart that ±50%
produces.

New: `random_float().mul_add(0.4, 0.8)` maps U[0, 1] to U[0.8, 1.2]
— a ±20% band. Still enough phase drift to bust a synchronised
reconnect storm but tight enough that batching-across-clients at
low attempt counts isn't destroyed.

## Test updates

lib.rs test module:
  - max_reconnect_delay_value: 60 → 30.
  - New: min_reconnect_delay_value (== 100ms).
  - New: min_reconnect_delay_is_below_default (MIN < DEFAULT so a
    caller that never touches initial_delay gets the 1s default,
    not the floor).
  - New: min_reconnect_delay_below_max.
  - Rename max_reconnect_delay_is_one_minute → _is_thirty_seconds.

reconnect.rs test module:
  - jitter_bounds assertion: 500ms..1500ms → 800ms..1200ms.
  - zero_backoff_multiplier_no_op: attempt 0 stays at 1s, attempt 1+
    floors to MIN_RECONNECT_DELAY (was 0ms, now 100ms).
  - negative_backoff_multiplier test comment updates to match the
    new floor-after-clamp behavior.
  - A handful of timing-bound assertions adjusted to the new bounds.

## Behavior impact for downstream callers

Any caller that was constructing a WsConfig with initial_delay ==
ZERO will see 100ms waits starting on the next reconnect attempt.
That's an observable improvement (no hot loop) and the grep sweep
confirms no caller relies on the zero-wait semantic; all uses pass
finite delays through.

Tracks br-y54mi.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
